think most, almost every job I've had, I think I almost always started as like a freelancer, came in as a contractor and worked there for a while before joining full time.

Especially since, congratulations, I know you just raised a round of funding, which is super exciting, and I'm sure a moment of big growth for the company.

Yeah, yeah.
I think like, so we've just approached things so differently, I think, from most.
I think almost every player overall, like, starts with software.
Some have a little bit of domain expertise and content and growing, but most don't.
Like, if you look at the founders of most companies, like, they're trying to, like, some of this stuff.
And so for us, we just started with service because it was just like, as a CMO, I was trying to build the thing I wanted myself.
And it was just like, happened very organically, which was like, hey, in my last company, I started building these workflows, and it really helped.
These are the, this is like, embedding all the.
Ways I thought about every single step of a process, right?
Like of going from idea to published.
And I had done this for 15 years and built it in every company I worked for.
So it wasn't like a thing that was just like, oh, let me hack this together for the first time in my life.
was more like, no, no, no.
I've been struggling with the stuff for years.
Like I remember back at IBM and HP hiring like four managing editors, doing 20 pieces of content a week, having a contributor network of over a thousand contributors.
Like that  is hard to manage.
Like there was like a lot of steps.
Like at any given point in time, had a hundred assignments between ideation and ready to publish.
Like that is like legit.
You go through and then you're spending $2 a word on some content.
It's like, you got to get that content right.
Your brief has to be good.
Like, and then you start coming back and your editors are like, oh man, all the content is way off or it's not doing well.
It's like, we just spent three grand on this piece of content.
It better do well.
Why is it not doing well?
And training team.

And so then, like, as I built it, it's like, it was just so obvious, like, I don't want to buy a tool that because I knew from my experience hiring editors, like, you're, you're either like, know how to think about your audience and grow, or you're really good at like the art and science of content, mostly the art, but very rarely, you know how to do both of like thinking about your audience and serving your audience and being really amazing.
at like raising the bar for content.
And so I'm like, if you give a tool to any, anyone out there, they're going to fail no matter what, no matter how good the tool is, you know, and so the service just made sense.
Now, with that being said, I think one of the challenges is just like, how do you solve for capacity while maintaining and improving quality?
And while I'm figuring out like the right balance between, you know, feeling like white glove service, but also not overdoing and being too flexible with saying yes,
Like recently, one of our customers asked us to do like social posts and the team ended up saying yes, which was, I think was a mistake.
And then ended up distracting them from the core of what we do.
And then the customer is like, oh, the quality is not good over here.
And it's like, yeah, because we didn't calibrate because we were saying yes to 12 other things.
Right.

Like, so that still is like part of the challenge is like when you're doing service, you still need to figure out like how to sell a strategy, sell the work you're doing, show a little bit of the work.
So they understand and have empathy for all that you put in, you know.
And so that's mostly like what kind of keeps me up at night a little bit.
It's just like, how do we bring in amazing people that are thinking AI native and building leverage, not just like, I'm just going to do 10 pieces of content, ship it out and call it a day.
It's more of like, no, no, no.
Like you're trying to win trust with that customer's audience, you know, by delivering overwhelming value day in and day out.
And so.
So now it's like the platform gets, it's getting better.

And it's more about how do we figure out what is the right delivery people model, essentially, that it's more of kind of like a Palantir kind of model where you have a platform and you all are using the platform to deliver the service.

And we'll start to expose more and more of the platform to customers for dashboards or them to see work or to approve or comment on things, you know, and then over time, let them kind of like the whole team use it.
And so that's more of kind of where we're going.
But then we're also seeing that everything we're building, there's components of it that can be highly monetized.
Like, for instance, like the LLM tracking piece, like can be a standalone thing, you know, because it's a hot category right now, and then the infrastructure as well.
And so there's like a lot of adjacent services, different services that we can tackle that we're using it for ourselves internally.
Then there's like software monetization.
And then there's like infrastructure monetization, but then there's also just like scalability things because we're not demand constraints.
So we have more demand that we can handle essentially, you know.
Yeah, it's interesting.
I think watching Tribe had a lot of those questions early, which is like, how do you scale a services business?
So it's interesting to hear how, you know, you're thinking about it.

The platform is really interesting in doing my written evaluation and then talking to your team afterwards because some of the challenges that I had doing it are clearly things that you've solved or like getting close to solving using the platform itself, which is very cool.
Because a lot of it is like writing stuff where it's like that last 20% or whatever to get it really good.

feels like the really challenging part, getting it that like last 20%, getting it really good and really tight and well.

Yeah, so it's interesting.
When I was, when the, when your recruiter first reached out, I sort of said that I'm not actively looking for a full-time job.
I think, which is true, I've done a lot of freelancing for companies.
And so I have had a lot of time to think about, you know, what I'm interested in next.
And I think especially in this world of, you know, AI and all these companies doing it, but like not sure how to get value from it.
And having worked at companies at all different stages, I had some thoughts about kind of what I wanted to be doing and where I wanted to be doing.
And I think one mistake I made early on was working at companies.
that hadn't quite found product market fit yet, that were like really excited about doing content.
They had this, you know, hypothesis that could help grow the business.
So like coming on, but when they didn't have a strong enough sense of who their customer was and what problem they were solving, there was just like so much churn, right?
You they invest in content, but then by the time it's out there in the world, they like pivoted to do something else.
And it feels like you're kind of constantly in this position where you're trying to like explain why things don't work, or you're like undoing the work you just did.
So I knew I wanted to be at a company that felt like it really had nailed who its customer was.
They knew what problem they were solving.
That was sort of like number one thing.

And I think the other thing is there's also so much out there where people just, it's like now that they have LLMs to create content, it's like they think they can just, they just want to churn it out and they think that that's like how it works and how you get value from it is just like.
More, more, more.
And I feel like I see that more and more out there.
People who really don't understand, like, the quality is important, too.

So I think a company that felt like they wanted to use AI, but were really dedicated to creating, like, valuable content, content that knew what its goals were, content that was good, and trying to do this in a scalable way.

So honestly, when, yeah, when Marilia reached out and sort of said we're looking for someone with a background in creating content, but who's sort of more process-minded, I feel like it was like a little, like, light bulb went off in my head.
Because I think that is what I'm interested in doing in this next phase, is not just creating the content myself, because it feels like as a, we're moving away from that being the model, right?
But to figure out how to sort of be the human in the loop to how to create good and valuable content that's accomplishing.

goals at scale and being part of a project like that felt exciting because I felt like I could have a much bigger impact than I can being embedded anywhere on my own as, you know, like a copywriter or a content creator or even.
And I think also I've worked for so many companies on marketing teams where you're like sort of sitting on top of the product, but you're not working in the product itself.

And so this feels like kind of exciting because as a marketer and a content creator, it's like an opportunity to be sort of working within the product and helping create the product, which is something that I really had the opportunity to do.
So that felt really unique as well.
Yeah, exactly.
And it's like, and it's why even for the role, like we only assign about 80% of the cost of these roles to COGS.

I know it's like not to get into financial things, but, but it's like, but part of it is like really to say like 20% at least should be, or if not more like R&D and product management and feedback and.

And I think some of our most successful people are the ones that write the best, like, ticket is like a bad word for a bit, but it's like the best requirements or give the best feedback to engineering on like, hey, this is where I'm getting stuck.
This is what I'm trying to accomplish.
This is how I think this should be.

But they're also the ones that have, like, really good taste on, like, it's almost like you need to play with models enough to know where the edge is and where it falls short and what the levers you have to get it to a good place.
But also know, like, as a new model comes out, like, where it's like, oh, that thing I couldn't do last week.
And now this is actually way better now, like on this.

Or, hey, this thing is like, don't even try to do it this way.
Like, this is just not going to work, you know, like, it's going to fail in all these other ways.
And, like, understanding, like, the edges of those things and how to shape it, but understanding as it's a.

think that's one of the best things about it.

What has energized you in your work?
And then the opposite of that is also like, what has usually like drained your energy, you know, past roles or past gigs and things that you've done, you know?
I mean, I think personally, when I feel like I'm learning, I think there's like, when I feel like I'm learning new things in a role that feels really energizing to me.

So when you're sort of operating at the edge of your own experience, it's not, was like a super comfy place to be, but it's sort of the most interesting when you feel like you're like learning every day.

And I think in terms of what's draining, I think I've worked at a lot of companies that like were, you know, if process is a spectrum, companies that are at the one end where there's just like no process, nothing's repeatable.
You waste so much time.
And then companies that I feel like I've gone so far in the other end where like every Asana subbed.
Fathom like 12 subtasks that you have to check out.
So there's so much process.
And then it's like your calendar is just full of, you know, standing meetings so that no one has time to actually get any work done.
Companies that have gone so far the other end where it feels like you're just like due process all day instead of actually like accomplishing anything.
Yeah.
I think those, that sort of the way that companies work, those two ends of the process of that sort of process spectrum feel like the things that I find the most draining.
And like, I think at early stage startups, lack of process is just the way it is.
But I think some companies, there's like, there's no interest in investing in changing that.

And I think that's hard because it feels like everyone's just like running around reacting to everything all the time without sort of taking a step back to think about why they're doing something and like what they should be doing.
So I think for me personally, those are kind of the areas that I find exciting.
And draining and are very much about like the way companies work and the way teams work.
Yeah, that makes sense.

I think like it's always a tough balance, but the way I kind of think about it a little bit is you got to do the zero to own to figure out how to achieve great and then work backwards on what are the parts of that that should be standardized a little bit so that like we're consistently hitting great and not trying to reinvent the wheel every time.
Unless we're intentionally trying to reinvent the wheel or intentionally trying to figure something else out or solve for something, right?
Like it's like don't do something completely different unless you are the top expert on the thing you're about to do or there's a very good reason why you're doing it differently, a.k.a.

you're trying to improve something or change something, you know, but like just doing something different for the sake of doing different is like when you know something works really well is sometimes counterproductive when you're trying to scale fast, right?
Like, yeah.
But then trying to standardize before you know what works is also pretty bad, too, you know, like, so it's like, yeah.
You know, it's interesting.
I often find that the fully remote companies, like, do a better job, I think.
A, because people are working in different places, so you have to have the communications process in place.
And then because people are in different time zones, like, you're forced to solve your own problem, like, more often.
Because you can always wait for whatever your team member in Western Europe to wake up for the day.
So I think the companies, like, Animals, the content marketing agency I worked at for a while, they had, like, a very defined process for communications, for how projects run.
And I think part of it was because they were that fully remote team.
It had to work that way.
It had to work or nothing would get done.
And, like, in the spectrum of, like, heads down creating versus, like, talking to customers, like, how do you, like, see yourself, like, in an ideal world, you know?
Yeah.
I mean, I think I enjoy...
Both.

I think most of the contact I've had with customers in previous roles have been more like gaining insights, if that makes sense, much more like conversations for things like trying to figure out, you know, how they saw their product, how they saw their ideal audience.
I think digging to get insights because there are so many things that come out in a conversation, like not everyone's like a writing person, right?
Like you can try to get them to like fill out questionnaires, but I found that it doesn't always work that way.
So I think those conversations I've always really enjoyed because it feels like you're trying to get those insights that will make the output better.
But I also, I mean, I do enjoy focused time to, to work, whatever that is.
And I feel like writing sometimes.
And then also just, I mean, I was talking about having done the, the take-home exercise.

Totally went down the rabbit hole trying to figure out how to use GPT for it, which I think was a mistake because I hadn't really used GPT-5 much yet, but totally going down the rabbit hole trying to figure out how to get it to do what I wanted to and also why I wasn't getting the result I want to.
So that to me feels like the head down work that feels, it's like solving a puzzle almost.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
That's how I think about it too.
Yeah, I think like the people that have been like really successful here, I think, are ones that kind of find the right balance of that.
But also in some ways, even for me and others, like we're involved in a lot of customers, right?

Like, and so there's a little bit of like time bounding yourself a little bit because you couldn't spend infinite time with just one customer or just like going super deep and like just staying there forever, right?
Like, but also like it wouldn't be a sustainable.
Business if like you had two, three, four people per account going super deep and being full-time pretty much, right?

So I think that that's what some people have struggled with and others that have done well have like figure out a way to context switch, but also go deep enough and build leverage for themselves, you know, and kind of like, but I don't know, like how do you think about, because that sometimes is the change for a lot of folks, even folks that were freelancing before.

It's just like, oh, I'm dealing with three customers or four customers right now, not like one, you know, or, and it kind of- do you feel like the way that they've struggled is when you talk about going too deep, do you mean in the sense of like their contact with the customer, like being sort of at their beck and call?
It's more like the rabbit hole of like, this customer asked me this thing and I just spent the whole week trying to figure something out, you know?
Yeah.
And then I completely-
Really just like ignore the other three customers and I was super behind and now I'm like really struggling.

It's essentially like having, and this is mostly people that worked on one single company at a time for a long time, you know, where they're just like, oh, where's like people that worked at agencies before and like totally get it.
Cause they're just like, you know, if anything, they're like, oh, this is nice.
You're not asking me to manage 20 accounts.
But then people that work at a single company, they're like, wait, three, this is a lot.
Like, this is a lot of context.
I can't do it.

I mean, I think as someone who's freelanced and is sort of used to working on multiple projects, to me, it's about balancing sort of importance and urgency because all customers, like their problem is always like the most important thing for them.
And there's like always people coming to you with a sense of like, oh my God, the building's on fire.
And sometimes it is really urgent.
Like it's always important to them, right?
Right.
So, being able to recognize So,
And like how important something is, how urgent it is, that that's not always the same thing, right?
Something might be really important, but if it's not urgent, there might be something else that comes before it.
So I think just like ruthless time and project management is really what it comes down to.
And obviously you're not going to tell the client, you know, this is important, but it's not urgent.
It's not urgent to me because that's not how you manage it.
But I think recognizing that for yourself so that like you don't always feel like you're just putting out fires all the time.
And I imagine too, you know, within a team, really good communication is important too.
So you feel like, you know, that only works if you have the right priorities.
So making sure, you know, within a team, within your manager, that everyone's aligned on kind of what is, what is urgent and what is important and then figuring out how to balance that.
Yeah.
Okay.

And then happy to answer more questions too.
But like, what do you think is like driving you from a work perspective right now in life?

Yeah, I mean, I think some of the things I said before, I think with, as someone whose background is in writing, and then, you know, with the rise of LLMs, trying to figure out what feels like an interesting and sort of strategic next step.
Because I've spent my whole life being immersed in writing, and like recognizing good writing, and I've built up this whole skill set.

trying to figure out in this, you know, new world we're embarking on, how can I use that, not just in a strategic way, but in because like, that's also what I love to do, and like what I see value in.
So trying to find a way that feels true to that purpose, but also feels like that skill set would be useful in this kind of new world, and wanting to be a part of a company.
It's building something because I think that's something with a background and startups and tech that I've always enjoyed that feeling of, you know, when working full time.

think the thing I missed most as a freelancer, even when you're embedded somewhere for months at a time, it's not quite the same as working with a team where you really feel like you're building something together.

Um, so I think coming back to a full time role, that's what I was looking for, like that sense of purpose and, and being able to use my skill set in a way that feels like exciting and interesting, but, um, and that, and that valued that background and like good writing too.
Um, which, you know, I think it's, it's hard to find.
It feels like this position was unique and that it was sort of valuing someone who's like a more process oriented writer.
And I think looking at the people you have hired already, um, and what their backgrounds are, like the fact that you've achieved creative.

Officer who was, you know, a journalist for decades and, you know, content and writing themselves to me felt like a really big marker that your company is a place where you do like value not just like churning out more, but like really wanted to figure out how to make it good.
Yeah, yeah, that's awesome.
Well, happy to, if there's any other questions I can cover for you on the business or the teams or anything else too, yeah.

Yeah, I guess my biggest question for you would just be from like your perspective, I guess, specific to content operations since that's where I'd be working, but anywhere in the company, like what feels like it's going really well right now and what are some of, are there like pain points that you feel now and, you know, how do you feel like?
Yeah, yeah, think one thing that really improved and is going well now.
Is our process of onboarding customers, which we call the strategies and execution sprints.
And so it's like eight weeks.
What we found is like when we try to do 90 day and people perceive it as a pilot, they expected full ROI in 90 days.
And it was like a mistake.
And so we had a lot of people not convert into a 12 month contract after that.
Whereas like with the strategy sprint, like knock on wood, so far in the last like three months, 100% of people converted.
So then it's like...
100, that's a nice number.
Yeah, so we haven't, you know, again, knock on wood, I keep saying this and I feel like I should stop saying this because it's just going to jinx myself.
I think it's mostly because we just standardized those eight weeks and getting the artifacts, which is like the kind of the context that we have, right?
Doing a lot of calibration, aligning on the strategy, mapping the topics that the audience that we define for them cares about, like putting that into a content OS.
And then like aligning on how to prioritize those opportunities.

And then, you know, getting to publish by week four and then, like, starting to publish daily for them by the end of the sprint and, like, the signals, setting up all the analytics, doing all the stuff.
Like, it's all, like, kind of really good when we actually follow really closely.
So it's mostly now, like, a capacity thing of, like, how do we, but we're protecting the strategy sprint teams quite a bit, you know, to just keep it, like, really, like, high talent density.
So I think that's going really well.
And then I think on the delivery side, which is kind of, like, the pods where accounts go into after that, there's just a lot of accounts that never went through that process.
And it's kind of, like, a mixed bag sometimes.
I think they're mostly at a good place, but, like, expectation setting wasn't as strong before.
And, you know, like, so then there's some accounts that are still in the, like, they expect us to do everything for them.
They don't know where the line is of what we do and don't do for them.
Like, they, you know, the strategy might not be as crisp.
The artifacts that we created might not be as good.
then, like, people are doing way more manual work than they should be doing.
way they should more manual
Or, you know, like, or they're asking us to do these kind of crazy things outside and beyond.
So there's some of that, like, a lot less now.
But because of that, it's, like, really unclear until now where we're, like, tracking more things.

What, like, if someone's spending three, four hours on an output to get it to client-ready or publish-ready, are they adding four times more value than someone spending one hour, than someone spending 30 minutes?

And why is there that delta, you know, like, and where's the platform needs to be improved versus where the human is just, like, overthinking things where it's, like, or because there's not enough alignment with the customers or, you know, so that's where, like, the fuzzy part is of, like, what we need to keep figuring out, you know.
It's, like, how do we continue to create more leverage and efficiency for ourselves and how we deliver, but also, like, learn from it, you know.
So whereas versus just, like, I'm just going to do this manually and just get it over.
Yeah, yeah.
Versus,
No, no, that's not the point.
The point is not to just deliver it.
The point is to figure out how the next time we deliver it, we're better, faster, and more efficient.
Yeah, totally.
And I'm curious, in this role, you sort of mentioned that one of the things that people have struggled with in the past is balancing multiple clients.
Are there any other areas where you feel like people might find challenging or that you've recognized as a challenge in this role in particular?
Yeah, I think it's just like, we have this thing that I call the best hire mentality.
And the goal is for everyone to have this best hire mentality where like truly customers see it as like, you're my best hire I've ever made, even though you're not a full-time hire.
Like, I wish I could poach too.
I wish, you know, like, encompass that mentality.
And that usually means like you're approaching it now, like your job is not to like review 10 outputs and crank it out and call it a day.
Like your job is truly like to help your customers be successful.
And grow organically through content and identifying the right opportunities, gaining a deep understanding of their audience and, you know, understanding what's working, watching the signals.
And I think like people just sometimes might lose sight of that because they're so like in the weeds and don't plan that work, their work as much.
Whereas just like, I will always approach it as like, oh, okay.
Like I need to do X deliverables in order to meet expectations.
So how do I like batch these things so that like I'm doing deep work and I'm getting a little bit ahead?

Like if you're trying to like scramble like two hours before the weekly sync to publish your first three pieces of content for the week and finish producing them and like you feel super behind, like then you never catch up and just need to like figure out like how to get ahead of it, you know?
And if you don't, then you're just going to be all over the place and then like feel like you're always underwater.
Whereas like the people that have done that, they're like structure their day.
And figure out like how to get ahead of it.
